items.q: What does cultural relativism ask an observer to do?
items.correct: Understand a practice within its own cultural context rather than by outside standards.
items.misconception: Approve of and agree with every practice in every culture.
items.random_wrong: Rank cultures from most to least advanced.
items.why: Students confuse cultural relativism as a method of understanding with moral endorsement of all practices.

items.q: What does the principle of holism mean in anthropology?
items.correct: That parts of a society such as religion, kinship and economy are understood in relation to the whole.
items.misconception: That all human societies are essentially identical.
items.random_wrong: That anthropologists study only sacred rituals.
items.why: Learners mistake holism for a claim of sameness rather than an emphasis on interconnection within a society.

items.correct: Judging another culture by the assumptions and values of one's own.
items.misconception: A neutral, scientific way of comparing cultures.
items.why: Students fail to see ethnocentrism as a bias to be avoided, treating it as an acceptable comparison method.

items.q: Understanding a practice within its own cultural context rather than by outside standards is called:
items.why: Correct answer: Cultural relativism. “Ethnocentrism” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The principle that kinship, economy and religion must be studied as an interconnected whole is known as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Holism. “Specialisation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Judging another culture by the values of one's own culture is termed:
items.why: It's Ethnocentrism. “Relativism” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Anthropologists mainly communicate their findings through:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ethnographic monographs and accounts. “Laboratory reports” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
